article_title: Huaneng Connects China's Deepest Offshore Wind Farm to the Grid as 504 MW Shandong Peninsula North L Pushes Fixed-Bottom Limits to 56 Meters
summary: Two factual claims are misattributed to Maritime Executive — the source snapshot does not contain them. Fix attribution or remove the claims, then resubmit.

findings.message: Misattribution: 'combined drone-and-magnetic-field technique' for the 95.6 km submarine cable is attributed to Maritime Executive, but that phrase does not appear in the snapshot
findings.details: Maritime Executive confirms the 95.6 km cable distance but says nothing about a 'drone-and-magnetic-field' installation technique. Either find a source that actually states this and re-attribute it, or remove the claim. As written, it reads as an unsourced/hallucinated technical detail.

findings.message: Misattribution: '6 GW of new Chinese offshore wind commissioning in 2025, more than half of global additions' is attributed to Maritime Executive, but the snapshot contains no such figure
findings.details: The Maritime Executive snapshot does not provide any 2025 capacity totals or comparison to global additions. Either find the actual source for this statistic and re-attribute, or remove the sentence.

content_preview.title: Huaneng Connects China's Deepest Offshore Wind Farm to the Grid as 504 MW Shandong Peninsula North L Pushes Fixed-Bottom Limits to 56 Meters
content_preview.summary: China Huaneng Group fully energized the 504 MW Shandong Peninsula North L offshore wind farm on April 7, 2026, installing 42 twelve-megawatt turbines on jacket foundations in 52 to 56 meters of water — the deepest fixed-bottom offshore wind project ever built in China.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

China Huaneng Group has fully energized the 504 MW Shandong Peninsula North L offshore wind farm, the deepest commercial offshore wind installation ever built in China. Full-power grid delivery began on April 7, 2026, [according to Maritime Executive](https://maritime-executive.com/article/china-commissions-wind-farm-at-its-deepest-offshore-position), and the project is expected to generate roughly 1.7 terawatt-hours of electricity per year, displacing about 500,000 tonnes of standa...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (~700 words, within range). Clear sectioning (Overview / What We Know / Why It Matters / What We Don't Know / Context). Tone is neutral and appropriately tentative where the developer's figures are state-channel-sourced. Two internal cross-references to prior MH coverage (supercritical CO2 generator 2026-02-15, hydrogen turboprop 2026-04-14) verified — both articles exist at the cited slugs.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all 4 local snapshots from sources/2026-04/huaneng-connects-chinas-deepest-offshore-wind-farm-to-the-grid-as-504-mw-shandong-peninsula-north-l-pushes-fixed-bottom-limits-to-56-meters/ (all 200 OK, no Archive.org fallbacks needed). source-0.html (OffshoreWind.biz): VERIFIED — 52-56m depth, 42x12 MW turbines, 504 MW total, ~70 km off Shandong, 83.9m jacket foundations as tallest in China, BeiDou-based positioning with millimeter accuracy, pile-driving time reduced 48h to 29h. source-1.html (Maritime Executive): TWO MISATTRIBUTIONS — (a) the 'combined drone-and-magnetic-field technique' phrase for the 95.6 km submarine cable is NOT in this snapshot (the 95.6 km figure itself IS confirmed); (b) the '6 GW Chinese commissioning in 2025, more than half of global additions' statistic is NOT in this snapshot. The Seagreen 58.7m record and the April 7 grid-delivery date ARE confirmed. source-2.html (CGTN): VERIFIED — 1.7 TWh annual generation and ~500,000 tonnes coal displacement (CGTN says 'saving' rather than 'displacing'; equivalent meaning). Also independently corroborates 504 MW / 42x12 MW / 70 km / 52-56 m. Note: CGTN is Chinese state media, useful for confirming announced figures but not independent verification. source-3.html (Windtech International): VERIFIED — 1.7 TWh and ~500,000 tonnes coal explicitly stated; also corroborates 504 MW / 42x12 MW / 70 km / 52-56 m / 'deepest commercial offshore wind installation in China'.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Two errors detected, both attribution problems on a single source (Maritime Executive). The underlying claims may be true, but as written the article cites a source that does not contain them. This is a misattribution finding — the bot must either find and cite the actual source for each claim or remove the sentence. All other claims map cleanly to their cited snapshots.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Appropriately frames developer-supplied figures (output, coal-equivalent, jacket foundation specs) as 'expected', 'according to', 'per developer' rather than asserting them as independent measurements. The 'Why It Matters' section is analytical without being editorial. No AI self-reference, no sensationalism.
editor_notes.originality: Genuinely new topic. No prior Machine Herald coverage of Shandong Peninsula North L, China Huaneng's offshore wind portfolio, or fixed-bottom-depth records. The two internal links are to thematically adjacent but distinct stories (CO2 power generator, hydrogen turboprop) and provide useful editorial continuity rather than overlap.
concerns: Two factual claims are sourced to Maritime Executive but are absent from the Maritime Executive snapshot — must be re-attributed to their actual source or removed.

Three of the four sources (Maritime Executive, CGTN, Windtech International) are not on the source allowlist. They are reputable trade/news outlets but should be considered for addition to config/source_allowlist.txt to silence future warnings.

Heavy reliance on developer-supplied figures (Huaneng's own claims about generation, coal displacement, technical innovations) without independent technical corroboration — partially unavoidable for a state-owned-enterprise commissioning announcement, but the article handles this honestly with hedged language.
recommendations: Locate the actual source for the 'drone-and-magnetic-field' submarine cable installation technique. If it cannot be verified against a reputable source, remove that detail entirely.
Locate the actual source for the '6 GW in 2025, more than half of global additions' statistic (likely GWEC's Global Wind Report or a similar industry tracker). Re-attribute or remove.
Optional: add maritime-executive.com, news.cgtn.com, and windtech-international.com to the source allowlist if these are intended to be regularly used.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Solid energy/infrastructure piece with correct primary facts (capacity, depth, foundations, location, BeiDou positioning, pile-driving reduction). Two specific claims are misattributed to a source that does not contain them — those need correction before approval. Once the attributions are fixed (or the unverifiable details removed), this is publishable as-is.
